agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business and compliance decision with significant legal exposure.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Record Management / Data Lifecycle — intersects with Compliance, Customer Reporting, Dispute Resolution, Financial Auditing, and Customs/Trade bounded contexts",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete audit trail of a delivery: pickup, transit events, proof of delivery, signatures, timestamps, chain of custody. This is a legal document, not just operational data." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Evidence that goods were delivered to the correct recipient — signatures, photos, GPS coordinates, timestamps. Required for dispute resolution and contractual fulfillment." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The business and legal rules governing how long records must be stored and when they may be destroyed. Driven by regulatory minimums, not storage cost optimization." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The use of delivery data by business customers to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations — meaning our retention policy directly constrains their compliance posture." },
      { "term": "Chain of Custody", "definition": "The documented, unbroken sequence of accountability for a parcel from sender to recipient. Once deleted, it cannot be reconstructed." },
      { "term": "Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)", "definition": "A data subject's right to request deletion — but this right does NOT override legal retention obligations. GDPR explicitly exempts data required for legal compliance from erasure." },
      { "term": "Statute of Limitations", "definition": "The legal time window within which claims can be brought. In the UK this is typically 6 years for contract disputes; in many EU jurisdictions 3-10 years. Delivery records are evidence in such disputes." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records must be retained for the longer of: (a) the applicable statute of limitations for contract/tort claims in the jurisdiction of delivery, or (b) the minimum period required by tax/customs/trade regulations in that jurisdiction.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Premature deletion of delivery records destroys legal evidence. A court or tax authority can compel production of these records. If they do not exist, the company faces adverse inference, fines, or liability. This is a legal constraint, not a policy preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record is deleted before the maximum of all applicable legal retention periods for its delivery jurisdiction has expired."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "UK contract claims have a 6-year statute of limitations (Limitation Act 1980). Delivery records for UK deliveries must be retained for a minimum of 6 years from delivery date.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A customer or counterparty can bring a breach of contract claim up to 6 years after delivery. Without the delivery record, the company cannot defend itself. This is a hard legal constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a delivery completed in the UK, the delivery record exists and is retrievable for at least 6 years from the delivery date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "EU VAT regulations require retention of transaction-supporting documents (including proof of delivery for zero-rated cross-border supplies) for periods ranging from 5 to 10 years depending on member state.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Tax authorities can audit VAT for the full retention period. Missing PODs for cross-border deliveries can result in the zero-rating being denied and full VAT being assessed retroactively, plus penalties.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a cross-border EU delivery, delivery records including POD are retained for the minimum period required by the originating member state's VAT retention rules (typically 7-10 years)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "B2B customers who use delivery data for their own compliance reporting must have continued access to records for the duration of their regulatory obligations.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a contractual and commercial obligation rather than a direct legal mandate on the platform — but violating it would breach B2B service agreements and destroy customer trust. A VP could negotiate different terms, but cannot unilaterally delete data customers depend on.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a B2B customer with an active service agreement, all delivery records within the contractually guaranteed retention period are accessible via API or export."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Delivery records involved in active disputes, claims, investigations, or litigation holds must never be auto-deleted regardless of any retention policy.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Deletion of records subject to a litigation hold constitutes spoliation of evidence, which carries severe legal penalties including adverse inference, sanctions, and criminal liability in some jurisdictions.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a delivery record that is subject to a litigation hold or active dispute, automated deletion is blocked and the record is preserved indefinitely until the hold is released."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "GDPR does not mandate deletion of delivery records after 90 days. Article 17(3)(b) explicitly exempts data required for compliance with a legal obligation, and Article 17(3)(e) exempts data needed for the establishment, exercise, or defence of legal claims.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR is often misinterpreted as requiring aggressive deletion. In reality, retaining data needed for legal compliance or legal defence is explicitly permitted. Deleting records prematurely to 'comply with GDPR' while violating tax, customs, or limitation period requirements is legally incoherent.",
      "testable_assertion": "The data retention policy cites specific legal bases for each retention period, and does not use GDPR minimization as justification for deleting records still required under other legal obligations."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer requests delivery data from 18 months ago for a tax audit after 90-day deletion is implemented.", "expected_behavior": "Data no longer exists. Customer fails their tax audit. Customer pursues contractual remedies against the platform. Reputational and legal liability ensues." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer disputes a delivery 4 months after it occurred, claiming non-delivery.", "expected_behavior": "Under the 90-day policy, the POD and tracking data would already be deleted. The platform cannot prove delivery occurred, and must absorb the loss or face a chargeback with no evidence to dispute it." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC audits cross-border VAT zero-rating for deliveries from 3 years ago.", "expected_behavior": "Under the 90-day policy, all supporting PODs are deleted. VAT zero-rating is denied. The platform owes full VAT plus penalties on potentially millions of pounds in cross-border transactions." },
    { "scenario": "A slip-and-fall personal injury claim is filed 2 years after a delivery, alleging the courier caused property damage.", "expected_behavior": "Under the 90-day policy, the delivery record, GPS data, and courier assignment are deleted. The platform cannot identify which courier made the delivery or defend the claim." },
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer churns and demands a full data export before account closure.", "expected_behavior": "Under the 90-day policy, the majority of their historical delivery data is already gone. This likely violates the data portability provisions of the service agreement and GDPR Article 20." },
    { "scenario": "Customs authority in an EU member state requests proof of export for a delivery made 8 months ago.", "expected_behavior": "Under the 90-day policy, the record is deleted. The platform cannot prove the goods left the country. Import duty and penalties may be assessed." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE NEGATIVE. (1) VAT exposure: Cross-border VAT zero-rating depends on POD retention. Retroactive denial could cost millions. (2) Dispute losses: inability to prove delivery shifts all chargeback/dispute liability to the platform. (3) B2B contract breach: customers with compliance-dependent SLAs may claim damages. (4) Regulatory fines: tax authority penalties, potential HMRC/EU member state sanctions. Storage cost savings are trivially small compared to even a single VAT audit failure.",
    "operational": "NEGATIVE. Creates operational chaos: support teams cannot resolve delivery disputes older than 90 days, compliance team cannot respond to audit requests, legal team cannot defend claims. Forces B2B customers to build their own parallel record-keeping, degrading the platform's value proposition.",
    "user": "SEVERE NEGATIVE for B2B customers who depend on the platform as their system of record. Significant negative for B2C consumers who lose the ability to reference past deliveries for disputes or returns. Trust erosion across both segments.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL. This change creates legal liability across multiple regulatory regimes simultaneously: UK Limitation Act, EU VAT Directive, EU Customs Code, GDPR (paradoxically — deleting too early can also violate GDPR if it prevents fulfilling legal obligations), and bilateral B2B contract law. The risk is asymmetric: storage costs are linear and predictable; legal and regulatory penalties are nonlinear and potentially catastrophic.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Cannot fulfill their own regulatory obligations. Will demand contractual remedies or churn. Platform becomes a compliance liability rather than an asset." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "Lose ability to dispute deliveries, request proof of delivery, or reference order history beyond 90 days. Degrades trust and customer experience." },
      { "group": "Finance/Tax Team", "impact": "Cannot respond to VAT audits or customs inquiries. Directly exposes the company to retroactive tax assessments." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance Team", "impact": "Cannot defend contract claims, tort claims, or respond to litigation discovery. Spoliation risk on any claim filed after 90 days." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Cannot resolve disputes or investigate complaints for deliveries older than 90 days. Increased unresolvable tickets." },
      { "group": "Engineering Team", "impact": "Minimal positive impact from reduced storage. Potentially significant negative impact if they must later rebuild retention, archive, and retrieval systems under regulatory pressure." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 90,
    "reasoning": "The regulatory landscape for delivery record retention in EU/UK is well-established and unambiguous. UK Limitation Act (6 years), EU VAT retention requirements (5-10 years), and customs regulations all clearly exceed 90 days. The business impact is directionally certain and severe. Confidence is not 95+ only because specific member state requirements and exact B2B contract terms would need legal review to determine precise per-jurisdiction minimums.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK 6-year limitation period makes 90-day deletion illegal for dispute defence",
      "EU VAT retention requirements exceed 90 days in all member states",
      "B2B customers will be materially harmed",
      "Storage cost savings are negligible relative to regulatory exposure",
      "This is NOT merely an infrastructure change — it is a compliance decision"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact retention minimums per EU member state (varies 5-10 years)",
      "Specific B2B contract terms and SLA commitments around data access",
      "Whether the platform handles customs declarations that create additional retention obligations"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform processes cross-border EU deliveries subject to VAT zero-rating",
      "B2B service agreements include provisions for data access and retention",
      "The platform does not currently offer a separate long-term archival tier that would survive this deletion policy",
      "Delivery records include POD, tracking events, and associated metadata — not just a summary row"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but it fundamentally alters the platform's ability to meet legal obligations, defend claims, and serve B2B customers. This is a direct contradiction: deleting legally required records IS a business logic change. The framing is dangerously misleading and must be corrected before any implementation proceeds.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "B2B Customers", "Finance/Tax", "Executive Leadership"],
      "options": [
        "A) Reject the 90-day deletion entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B) Implement tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) to reduce costs while preserving records for the full legally required period",
        "C) Engage legal counsel to determine per-jurisdiction minimum retention periods and implement jurisdiction-aware retention policies"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B or C. The 7-year policy likely exists for good reasons. If cost reduction is the goal, move older records to cold storage (e.g., S3 Glacier) which reduces costs by 80-90% without destroying legal evidence. NEVER proceed with 90-day deletion without formal legal sign-off from counsel qualified in EU/UK regulatory law."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion destroys delivery records that are legally required to be retained for 5-10 years under EU VAT regulations and UK limitation periods.", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1, BR-2, BR-3" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion with no litigation hold exemption risks spoliation of evidence.", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "The change is mischaracterized as 'no business logic changes' when it directly impacts legal compliance, customer obligations, and dispute resolution capability.", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1, BR-4" },
    { "violation": "B2B customers who depend on the platform for compliance reporting will lose access to data they are contractually entitled to and legally required to retain.", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" }
  ]
}
